speaker: interviewer
text: It's Linda Burr. I'm interviewing Joseph Talpert in Johannesburg, South Africa. The date is June 13th, 1996, tape three. Joseph, you said that you had tried to escape once over the Pyrenees. Were there any other times when you, when you tried to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another time. But this wasn't an escape. It was authorized by the French authorities. They gave me permission to-- in fact, they wrote in the passport that I was allowed to use a military plane to go from Blagnac-- that's a place near Toulouse-- to Algiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this still while you were in Toulouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- in 1940. It was just when I came to Toulouse month or two afterwards. At the last moment, for reasons unknown to me, they changed their mind. They wouldn't allow me on the plane. And that when so far even that they gave me documents, for which they asked me to do them a favor, to hand it over to French authorities in Algeria. And that was the time I really thought I was gone. But nothing happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? Did they stop you at the airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stopped me at the airport. I was at the airport, a military airport-- military section. And they told me now there's been a sad change. And I'm not allowed to, to go on the plane.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who actually stopped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the French authorities at the airport, military authorities. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I had a black on white permission granted to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, getting back to the time that you were in Auvergne and the war coming to an end, were you aware that the war was actually ending? Did you know what was happening?

words.text: Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was aware that the war is coming to end, because we had the news that things were not going well for the German, that things-- that there might be-- the war might finish. Of course, I was rather pleased about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you notified that the war had ended? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, notified is everybody was-- it started at the post office. All stamps were marked, liberation. Liberation [INAUDIBLE] little post office. In fact, some people even made a lot of money off this, to tell a little incident, stamp collectors. They realized that if stamps were marked liberated, then some groups were very clever. They drove in all the little town, little villages. And they bought up the stocks of stamps. And I think they must have made quite a bit of money with that. And of course, the information, where it wasn't anymore that you were not allowed to listen to a radio. In the time of the German occupation, if you were caught listening to an overseas station, you were punished. And we knew, of course, that the war was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as soon as finished, I went to Paris. I took the first train, where I stayed for a little while. And then I went back to Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Paris? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris for a while, I worked with some American military group. It was a transport unit. And I worked for a little while there. Then I decide I go back to Belgium, see what's happening. Because I wanted to know what's happening to my family. I didn't have that information. I was still hoping that they might be alive, because I never had a-- the only notification I had was I heard that my brother was dead in water. Some friend of his came around me and gave me that information. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then of course, I was more than keen to go back to Belgium to see what's with the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, when you first arrived in Paris after the war, what was the atmosphere like? What was it like being in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the atmosphere was fantastic. Everybody was happy and-- in fact, V-Day, and I happened to be under, in a Metro just by the opera, in the Metro. I was nearly squashed to death. You couldn't move in the, in the passengers there-- tremendous crowd there was. And everybody was celebrating.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed for a little while in the, in the home what I rented.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a few months, I stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe the work that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was paperwork. It was checking their jeeps coming in and this-- all transport in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to get that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to see-- well, I haven't worked officially the whole time. I went to see if I couldn't get a job somewhere. There were some labor organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they placed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they placed me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you went back to Antwerp, where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my auntie was there, my Auntie Sophie. The one that was hidden in Belgium was there. I knew her address. I went there. And my-- and also, my other auntie from Morocco came shortly back afterwards. They were already back when I came to Antwerp. I came after their arrival with their family. And my other auntie was also back. But she was in Brussels with her family. Then I stayed with my Auntie Sophie there for awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your own apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no purpose to go back. It was occupied. Other people were staying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm afraid I didn't check on the name. But I went to look for the place. Well, there was nothing of our belongings anymore. They had no right to claim anything, because these were normal tenants staying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all your family's furniture and their belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, part was. But that woman, Mrs. [? Wentz ?] who said she had nothing of ours, but as I walked in, I recognized the furniture. That's a certain woman there when my parents were in hiding, who-- and she betrayed them when they came there. She put up her neck. She was crying and telling me what happened to them. I believed her at that time. It's only when I saw the furniture and other things there that I realized there's something not right. And then I discovered whatever she told me was wrong. Her daughter had a German soldier boyfriend, an officer. I think they were guilty [INAUDIBLE]. And, and I went to a lawyer. It was no purpose they claimed, because they would declare themselves insolvent. And I couldn't do a thing. The only thing I tried to arrange that they should return some furniture. It was returned to one of my aunties when I was on my way to South Africa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what work did you do while you were in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was in Antwerp, I tried to establish some contacts with some of our suppliers. And, and I managed to get some supply of fabrics from them, which I sold wholesale till I left Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, did you ever find out what had happened to your family, to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my family, yes-- well, I got the information that they all were-- they passed away in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, I knew that-- well, this I knew already before in Warsaw, that he died from typhus. And this, in fact, was reconfirmed recently in a couple of years ago when I got a notification that there was a newspaper coming out with-- in Belgium-- with, with an article from a certain man called Natan Renat, that he was with my brother when he died and that he had a message to hand over to the family. This was 50 years ago. And in fact, I managed to trace that man. And I went to see him in Belgium. And he told me the whole story, what happened really, that they were both deported to Auschwitz. [INAUDIBLE] redirected to Warsaw. There was a new camp for the German built there in 1943. And there was a-- in the camp, the inmates got typhus. And of course, the German were not very concerned supply medication. And very few survived. And one of the one who died was my brother. He was with his friend, Natan Renat. And they gave a message to, to try to trace the family if he survives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find Natan Renat?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I find?

speaker: interviewer
text: Natan Renat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found him when I got a article from the newspaper. And I tried to trace him through my family, but I couldn't, till eventually, we phoned the phone department in South Africa. And we asked them, could they trace anywhere in Europe a man by the name of Natan Renat. But they should start from Antwerp. Within 10 minutes, they phoned us back. They've got two phone numbers from that man in Antwerp. And of course, I found him right away. He wasn't available that day. The next day, I managed to talk to him. And I told him I'll come and see him in Belgium. what in fact I did very shortly afterwards. We went to Belgium, in Antwerp. And he told us the whole story. And that Renat was very active. Because the, that picture came up, the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Schindler case-- and in Belgium, there was a movement to, to recapitulate what happened in wartime, to educate the people for what happened and what-- and he was asked, also within company with a few other people-- in fact, most of them non-Jews-- to make lectures all over in Belgium. And they were going to schools, university, all over, to tell people what happened in wartime. And not only this, he was also active in the museum that opened in Mechelen, what was the, the transit camp for all people who were arrested in Belgium. And they made a museum there, for which I haven't seen it. Because I wasn't there at that time. And he was very instrumental in getting the money and all the facilities to open that museum for all the victims in wartime. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And this, of course, was quite a moving, moving moment for us, for me to hear all about what happened to my brother and other people also, in fact. That camp was in Warsaw. I think it was called a street, [NON-ENGLISH] Street. It was a new camp what they built at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, how long did you stay in Antwerp after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Antwerp from about two years, less than two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the atmosphere like in Antwerp? What was the attitude of the general population to Jews who had survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they're quite friendly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you know how people are, you know? They-- everybody cares about himself, you know? And, and everything has to go back to normal. And people are concerned about their own living. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I won't say they're special pro-Jewish family of people who used to sympathize with the Jews. But that's how far it went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you want to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: What made me leave? A relation [INAUDIBLE] from Johannesburg managed to trace me. And he came to Antwerp and made an issue that I should come to South Africa. In fact, he even booked my boat ticket [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and suggest I should come. And after all what happened to the family, I was looking for a future somewhere else, if possible. And this made me accept this offering. I left Belgium to come to South Africa in April, '47, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find it when you got here? What was your reception like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the family reception-- I had a little bit of family, cousins, there. I wasn't excited. But the country, it was totally different from Europe. And it took me some-- quite a while to adapt myself. Especially Sunday was-- when we had that day, I couldn't stand Sunday at that time. You know, coming as a single man, you know, Sunday was-- I found it most unpleasant. But eventually, I got to adjusted. And I started liking it. And eventually, I got married in Johannesburg in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find most difficult about adjusting to South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time when I arrived, I had no motor car. Transport was a problem. And I had to rely on other people to make friends. Then it took me some also-- earning wasn't so good at the beginning, either. Then it took me some time to come straight. And I found it very quiet. Activities at that time, I wasn't interested in sport. I might have been interested in sport activity, but I didn't participate. And I didn't know much, much what to do about it. And then it takes some time for adjustment. And-- but eventually, I change my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak English well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I spoke English. But I wouldn't say well. I spoke English. French was my language. But well, language wasn't a problem, really. My English was good enough to, to go around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay when you first arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with a cousin of mine and settled in in Johannesburg till I took my own flat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then life changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to some party. I think it was an engagement party. And then I met her there. We got acquainted. We got engaged very soon afterwards and also married in a short time afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Joseph, tell me about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we have four children. My first child was a daughter then two sons. And the latecomer was my, my daughter Suzanne, what was-- came much later than all the other kids. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's my-- it was my baby daughter. In fact, she's getting married now, the 1st of September. And we are looking forward to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, what work have you been doing since you've been in South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I've done work? I joined my cousin who had a clothing factory. First, I was working. Then I started traveling and then eventually built up my own business, what was oversea agencies and also import, mainly in the textile field. And that's what I've been doing up till, up till now, more or less. I'm 99% retired. I do a little bit work still, not too much, [INAUDIBLE] agency.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, how do you think that your, your wartime experiences have influenced your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't a pleasant period-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what has definitely affected me in a, in a way that I don't see life [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the easy way it was before. After all, what happened, it's more than bad memories, family memories. And I don't accept the attitude toward Germany. I reckon the Germans are guilty, not a few what claimed to be responsible or don't claim to be responsible. And it has left a bad taste for me, even the few times I had to go to Germany at some shows. I didn't enjoy being in Germany. I know the German language. I try to avoid using it. Good In fact, I lost, I lost quite a bit of my vocabulary, because I try avoiding speak German. Because there is definitely the hate feeling in me against the German. But I, I can't forgive them after what happened. And of course, it so left a big mark on my life. Of course, matters changed once I got married, you know, take a new interest in life, family-wise and all this. And the change of attitude in life or not, but this-- I can't forget what happened in wartime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, is there some message that you want to pass on about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only message I would like to pass on [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] that people should take life as it comes. Make the best of it. I reckon life is short. And you never know what can happen sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Joseph, who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first person is my grandfather. It's my father's father. And next to him, that's my father's mother, my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were staying in Belgium but originally came from Russia. This couple is my father and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel and Gittel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Watch the numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself, about the age of four years old. In fact, I had a problem later. These curls, I wanted to get rid of them. That's, I suppose, the reason why at this stage, I haven't got any curls at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Antwerp, in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home? In a studio?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the studio, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken at a seaside resort in Belgium. On the right is my brother, Maxi. And on the left is my cousin, Maxi Weinstein. And below is my other little cousin, Charlotte Weinstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your cousins, Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately. they've all been deported and died in concentration camp. OK? This is a photo of various cousin. The first one is my brother, Maxi Talpert. In the middle is a cousin, Grisha Kowalski. On the right side is my other cousin, Maxi Weinstein. Below is my little cousin, Charlotte Weinstein and her brother, Isaac Weinstein. On this photo on the left is my Auntie Maria, her husband, Herman. The surname is Schnur. And on the right side is their daughter, Irene.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photograph taken, Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken after the war in Antwerp. This photo and card is French identity papers which were made especially for me at a different name, Thibault, Joseph. I bought that card. But some friends helped me to have it stamped at the mayor's place to make it legal. This is a normal procedure in France. In all little places, you buy the card in a special stationery shop. And you fill it out. And you have it legalized by the, the authority, the French authority.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, it's stamped Dantras?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dantras, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were not there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I've never been there. Now, I realize. Because this is a different area. Gers is the, the province.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think somebody in the underground helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, somebody helped me there, yeah. Cause if I had it done, I had done in [INAUDIBLE]. And this wasn't done in [INAUDIBLE]. No, I signed it. It's my handwriting. This is a photo for myself, taken in 1946 for passport purpose.
